coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot supported bimetallic nanocomposite for the hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Jayasmita
Chung, Jin Suk
Hur, Seung Hyun
coredata.dc:description: The design of non-noble metal-based nanocomposites for the evolution of hydrogen gas in energy conversion devices is important ongoing research. In this study, carbon dot (CD) based bi-metallic hydroxide nanocomposites were synthesized for the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction. A series of nickel-cobalt double hydroxide nanocomposites were synthesized using a CD support. The structural features of the nanoparticles were analyzed through proper microscopic studies. The overpotential was −234 mV vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−25 mV with respect to RHE) for the hydrogen evolution reaction at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The Tafel slope was calculated to be 47 mV dec−1. The catalytic properties were attributed to the intrinsic layered structure, interconnected nanoarray configuration, and unique redox characteristics. CDs, with significant surface defects provided a higher active area for catalysis and electronic environment. This study highlights the use of a CD support for the synthesis of low-cost metal hydroxide electrocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot supported bimetallic nanocomposite for the hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of non-noble metal-based nanocomposites for the evolution of hydrogen gas in energy conversion devices is important ongoing research. In this study, carbon dot (CD) based bi-metallic hydroxide nanocomposites were synthesized for the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction. A series of nickel-cobalt double hydroxide nanocomposites were synthesized using a CD support. The structural features of the nanoparticles were analyzed through proper microscopic studies. The overpotential was −234 mV vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−25 mV with respect to RHE) for the hydrogen evolution reaction at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The Tafel slope was calculated to be 47 mV dec−1. The catalytic properties were attributed to the intrinsic layered structure, interconnected nanoarray configuration, and unique redox characteristics. CDs, with significant surface defects provided a higher active area for catalysis and electronic environment. This study highlights the use of a CD support for the synthesis of low-cost metal hydroxide electrocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para:
Recent global energy crisis can be addressed using alternative energy sources. In this respect, water would be an important resource because of its abundance in nature [1]. Water splitting produces sustainable hydrogen gas which with high energy density and efficient energy carrier property can act as efficient fuel [1]. Hydrogen generation can also be achieved through certain other methods apart from water splitting, such as thermal cracking, coal and biomass gasification, pyrolysis, and electrolysis but they are hazardous and expensive [2]. The catalytic water splitting being an eco-friendly production process, that allows the evolution of hydrogen through a clean and simple way [3], is widely adopted method. Since, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) follows a simple proton electron-transfer mechanism; it is widely studied electrocatalytic reaction. The HER follows a two-step pathway, either a Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer-Tafel mechanism depending on the reaction medium and nature of catalyst. In acidic medium, the catalyst (Cat)-mediated steps are as follows [4]:
(1) H3O+ + e-→ Cat-H + H2O --- Volmer step
(2) Cat-H + H3O+ + e− → H2 + H2O --- Heyrovsky step
(3) Cat-H + Cat-H → 2Cat + H2 --- Tafel step

The Cat-H bond strength is very important in the reaction. The catalyst should be strong enough to adsorb proton from water in the first step while labile to release the proton for H2 generation in the second step. Noble metal (Pt. Ir, etc.) based catalysts fulfilled such condition and provided lower overpotential and high current density. Hence they were popular choice for designing efficient electrocatalysts in acidic medium. However, their activity is limited to alkaline media, low availability, and associated toxicity [5,6]. In alkaline medium, the reaction becomes complex due to interaction of hydroxide ion on the catalyst surface and the sluggish kinetics at the counter electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction [7]. Therefore, a continuous search for alternative low cost but highly efficient catalysts that can work over a wide range of pH is ongoing. Recently, non-noble metal based, carbon nanoparticle-supported metal-based and metal-free carbon-based catalysts have attracted considerable attention [8–12]. Despite the different approaches towards the preparation of efficient catalysts, the stability, metal consumption and simple operation remains the main hurdle [3]. The non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts has attracted interest because of their activity in multiple pH medium and electrical conductivity [13]. Previously, different bifunctional electrocatalysts consisting of transition metals along with carbon based nonmetallic catalysts have been designed to achieve efficient HER [13–16].

Since, the first step of HER requires a metal with paired ‘d’-electrons to facilitate electron transfer and cleavage of the H–OH bond and the second step needs semi-empty d-orbital of catalyst for favorable Cat-H bond breakage [17], the transition metals would work as effective electrocatalysts with favorable arrangement of electrons at d-orbitals. Furthermore, it was observed that the bimetallic composites, possessing the facilities of improved electric conductivity, high hydrogen molecule adsorption-desorption and hetero-metal synergy to push the sluggish kinetics of the reaction [18], show better activities over monometallic catalyst materials. The electrochemical activities of bimetallic catalysts have also been explored for the oxygen evolution reaction, oxygen reduction reaction, HER, and Zn-air battery and supercapacitors [19–24]. It has been observed that the functionalities played an important role in controlling the catalytic activities. The different functionalities (carbide, phosphate, boride, sulfide, and oxide) of transition metals help them to perform as excellent electrocatalysts either in metal-metal composite or metal-carbon composite state [25]. In addition to normal oxide and hydroxide functionalities, layered double hydroxides (LDH) have more surface defects, chemical versatility and flexible structures that can be explored for electrocatalysis [26].

Previously, carbon-related materials have been reported to form composites with LDHs for electrocatalysis [27]. Of late, carbon dots have emerged as efficient supports for metal electrocatalysts with their unique optoelectronic properties and by providing higher surface area [28,29]. CDs are low cost, chemically resistant and stable. The particles can be distributed homogeneously in water. The surface functional groups at the surface of the CDs can coordinate with the d-orbitals of the metals [30]. The synergistic interaction within the CDs and metal components affect the catalytic activity of the as-synthesized catalyst.

It has been found that nickel (Ni) and cobalt (Co) based catalyst materials showed efficient electrochemical behavior due to their favorable electronic arrangement. Henceforth, in this study, the bimetallic Ni–Co nanocomposites have been synthesized with the aid of CDs. The suitable electronic behavior and surface defects of CDs have been utilized to stabilize and improve the performance of the bimetallic composite. The CD-supported Ni–Co double hydroxide composites were synthesized using a simple low temperature hydrothermal method in a solution phase reaction. The products were separated as precipitates of the solution phase reaction and oven dried for further experiments. Three different catalysts were prepared at different Co and Ni compositions. The CD-based Co–Ni double hydroxide-based electrode materials have not been used for HER. The structural features of CD/CoNi double-hydroxide composites have been investigated using a range of microscopic techniques. During characterization it was observed that both +2 and + 3 oxidation states were present in the composites. Presence of tetravalent metal provides high H2O absorption energy. Moreover, the catalytic activity of nickel and stability of cobalt provided enhanced activity [31]. In alkaline medium, the proton source is H2O, but the activation energy is higher. This leads to complicated reaction mechanisms in alkaline media compared to acidic media [3]. On the other hand, if the acidity of the medium is too higher, it might produce acid fog that contaminates the hydrogen produced [32]. Therefore, the HER has been performed using the 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and the activity has been checked at different medium pH. In addition, the KOH (0.1 M) electrolyte has been used to assess the catalyst activity towards the HER. A composite with a higher Co content exhibited significant stability and activity towards the HER with an overpotential of −234 mV and −340 mV at 10 mA cm−2, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−25 mV and −131 mV, respectively with respect to reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE) in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.1 KOH electrolyte. In the KOH electrolyte, however, the catalyst with a higher Ni content showed better HER activity with overpotential of −328 mV at 10 mA cm−2, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−119 mV with respect to RHE).
The chemicals and characterization part can be found in the Supplementary Materials.

Immensely fluorescent CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of triammonium citrate (TAC) and tetraethylpentamine (TEPA). Typically, a 1 M TAC solution was mixed with a 1 M TEPA solution at a 2:1 ratio in a 20 mL vial. The solution was treated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 4 h. After the reaction was complete, the as-obtained opaque solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r.p.m for 15 min to remove the large particles. The process was repeated for three times for complete removal of large particles. This solution was then dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h to remove unreacted particles. This solution, with intense blue emission, was then used to synthesize the metal composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot supported nickel-cobalt nanocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dot-supported bimetallic nanocomposites (CDCoNi) were prepared by a hydrothermal technique using the as-prepared CDs (through the above-mentioned method), nickel (II) acetate tetrahydrate, and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate as precursors. Typically, 0.1 M Ni(II) and Co(II) salt solutions were added at different ratios to 200 μL of a CD (1 mg mL−1) solution. The mixture was kept for hydrothermal treatment for 4 h at 200 °C. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged and washed with ethanol to obtain a solid precipitate. The product then dried at 240 °C for 6 h. The as-synthesized products were labelled, CDCoNi-1, CDCONi-2, and CDCoNi-3 in relation to the varied Co(II) and Ni(II) concentration. In another set of reactions, CoNi, CDNi, and CDCo nanoparticles were also prepared using the above-mentioned reaction path.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of CDCoNi-n (n = 1,2,3) towards the hydrogen evolution reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical measurements and catalytic activity of the CDCoNi-nanocomposites were examined using a three electrode cell in an electrochemical workstation (BioLogic, Science Instruments), taking a glassy carbon electrode (geometric area 0.0707 cm2) coated with the as-synthesized catalyst as the working electrode, platinum wire with a diameter of 1 mm as the counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl (in 3 M NaCl) electrode as the reference electrode. The working electrode was cleaned and dried properly, and 7 μL of the catalyst suspension (catalyst suspension was prepared by taking 1 mg compound on 1 mL solution of 5 vol% nafion solution in isopropanol) was then deposited and dried. This resulted in a 0.1 mgcm−2 catalyst loading. For studies of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was taken as the supporting electrolyte. After passing N2 gas in the solution, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was investigated by sweeping the potential from 0.0 to −0.7 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1 in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution under stirring at 1000 r.p.m. The potentials can be converted to those vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the following equation [33],
(4) E(RHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.059.pH + E0 (Ag/AgCl) V at 273K ---

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted within the frequency range of 105 Hz–0.1 Hz with a 5 mV amplitude at a bias potential of −0.62 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). A chronoamperometry curve was obtained at a selected potential of −025 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 10 h. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure the accuracy of the results.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to understand the surface chemical composition and different bonding configuration of the elements present in the as-synthesized CDCoNi-n particles. The broad range survey revealed the presence of elemental C1s, O1s, Ni2p, and Co2p for CDCoNi-n particles (Fig. S2). For CDCoNi-1 particles, deconvolution of the C1s peak revealed the presence of peaks at 285.5, 288.1, and 291.6 eV that were assigned to sp3 hybridized C–C, C–N, and OC–O bonds, respectively [44–46]. The O1s peaks were deconvoluted to obtain three peaks that corresponded to OC, -O-H, and O–C bonds. The Gaussian peak fit method provided a satellite peak at 862.5 eV and two sets of doublet peaks for the two spin states of Ni2p. The Ni 2p spectrum was best fitted by considering two spin-orbit doublets at 857.4 eV and 855.1 eV, as well as at 874.1 and 880.4 eV. These peaks were assigned to the 3/2 spin state of the +3 and + 2 oxidation state and the 1/2 spin state of the +3 and + 2 oxidation states of Ni, respectively [47–49]. Peak analysis of Co2p presented two sets of spin-orbit doublets at 781.8 and 774.9 eV for Co2p3/2 (+3 and + 2, respectively) as well as at 797.6 and 786.4 eV for Co2p1/2 (+3 and + 2, respectively). The satellite peak appeared at 803.3 eV [21,49–51]. Fig. 3 presents the XPS spectra of CDCoNi-1. Similar peaks were present in the CDCoNi-2 (Fig. S3) and CDCoNi-3 (Fig. S4) nanocomposites. XPS indicated the presence of hydroxide functionality in the three composites. XPS analysis of the CDs was also performed separately (Fig. S5). The broad survey revealed the presence of C1s, O1s, and N1s peaks. Deconvolution of the C1 peak revealed the presence of C–C, C–N, and OC–O bonds at 285.1, 287.3, and 289.1 eV, respectively [52]. These peaks resembled the C1s peak present at the CDCoNi-n, indicating that the origins of C1s peaks in CD-metal composites are CDs. The deconvolution of O1s of CDCoNi-1 revealed the presence of peaks at 531.05, 531.8, and 533.1 eV, corresponding to -O-H, O–C, and OC- bonds [53,54]. The -O-H peak was absent in CD. Multipeak fitting of the O1s peak of CDs revealed the presence of O–C and OC bonds. The presence of a -O-H peak indicated the formation of metal hydroxide supporting the XRD values. On the other hand, the slight deviation of the peak position and intensities was attributable to a metal-CD interaction. The chemical compositions of the CDs and CDCoNi-n were determined by the atomic %, as documented in Table S1. These structural features confirmed the association of CDs with the metal counterpart.

The structure and morphology of the as-synthesized CDCoNi-n particles were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). FESEM revealed the rough surface of the CDCoNi-1 particles. The top view exhibited the interconnected nano wall structure (Fig. 4 ). Such interconnected wall type morphology was observed for CDCoNi-2 and CDCoNi-3 (Fig. S6). On the other hand, the surface nature varied for the three composites. The morphology of the CDCoNi-n particles was elucidated further from the TEM images. Fig. 4B and C shows the TEM and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image with lattice fringes for CDCoNi-1. The selected area diffraction (SAD) pattern and HRTEM image indicated the crystallinity with distinct lattice parameters. Two lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.23 nm were present in CDCoNi-1. The lattice fringes were corresponded to (100) plan of carbon and the (101) plan for double hydroxide, respectively [55,56]. CDCoNi-2 and CDCoNi-3 (Fig. S7) also had a similar sheet-like structure. CDCoNi-2 possessed two lattice fringes at 0.21 nm [for carbon (100) plane] and 0.25 nm [for (101) plane of double hydroxide] [57], whereas CDCoNi-3 possessed lattice fringes at 0.21 nm [for carbon (100) plane] and 0.24 nm [for (101) plane of double hydroxide] [57]. The lattice fringe values for double hydroxides varied little. In XRD pattern, similar planes for the metals were obtained. Moreover, the TEM images showed that the morphology of the composites did not vary drastically with changes in the metal concentration. In addition, the low contrast within the particle images could not provide different grain boundaries. The CD particles, which are too tiny compared to metal sheet, could not be located separately in the sheet-like structure through the spectroscope. TEM of the as-prepared CD particles revealed a particle diameter of ∼7 nm (Fig. S8). The lattice fringe present in the CDs ∼ 0.21 nm is also found in the composites. This indicated the assembly of CDs in composites. The assembly of CDs with the metal composite was further confirmed from Raman spectral analysis. Fig. S9 shows that in the Raman spectra of CDCoNi-1 the D- and G-bands of CDs are present at 1350 and 1580 cm−1 along with M − OH band [23]. The peak over around 415 cm−1 was assigned to Ni–OH band; however, the peak for Co–OH could not be recognized. The ID/IG ratio was found to be 1.02 in the composite indicating predominance of surface defects of CDs over sp2 carbon domain. Similar peaks were obtained for CDCoNi-2 and 3 with varied intensity. However, Raman spectral analysis of CDs showed that the D-band was located at 1355 cm−1 but the G-band was broadened over a region of 1500–1800 cm−1 [58]. This might be due to overlap of G-and D′ band. This G band was found to be distinct in the composites indicating the interaction within metal composite and CDs. The ID/IG > 1 showed the predominance of surface defects during interaction within the CDs and metal hydroxides. Such interaction was used to improve the defect assisted charge transfer during electrocatalysis [59]. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas and pore-size distribution of as-synthesized of the metal composites were analyzed by recording the nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. S9). The adsorption-desorption curves were different for the three materials due to different multilayer adsorption or heterogeneous adsorption on non-porous adsorbents [60]. The measured BET surface areas for CDCoNi-1, CDCoNi-2, and CDCoNi-3 were 46.909, 48.071, and 37.304 m2 g−1, respectively. The pore volumes of the composites were 0.09, 0.09, and 0.01 cm3 g-1, respectively. The average pore radii of 3.8 nm, 3.6 nm, and 2.1 nm indicated the porous nature of the CDCoNi-1, 2, and 3, respectively. The results indicated that the as-synthesized composites possessed sufficient active sites for catalysis.

The electrocatalytic activity of the CDCoNi-n was examined in a N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution using a standard three-electrode configuration. An Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum electrode were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The bare glassy carbon electrode did not exhibit any HER activity with a negligible current density at −0.7 V. Fig. 5 presents the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the CDCoNi-n samples at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 without the iR-correction. The activity of the CDCo, CDNi, CoNi, CD, and Pt/C (10 wt % on carbon black) nanoparticles were measured within a potential window of 0 to −0.7 V under the same conditions. A catalyst loading of 0.1 mg cm−2 was used for all the catalysts. The onset potential of CDCoNi-1 (−175 mV) was smallest among the other catalysts apart from Pt/C (−119 mV) vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. The overpotential at a 10 mA cm−2 current density was considered as an important parameter for measuring the HER activity [61]. The reference catalyst, Pt/C, showed the low over potential of −190 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The CDCoNi-1 showed a lower overpotential of −234 mV, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, at a current density of 10 mAcm−2 than CDCoNi-2 (−311 mV, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode) and CDCoNi-3 (−300 mV, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode). When converted with respect to RHE, the overpotential values become −25, −102, and −91 mV, respectively. The comparative plot of the as-prepared nanoparticles and composites suggested that the HER activity depended on the metal nature and ratio.

The Tafel slope is a parameter indicating the HER mechanism. The Tafel slope (b) can be obtained from the following equation,
(5)
η = a + b
log
J
where η is the overpotential, a is the empirical coefficient, and J is the current density. The Tafel slope explains the electron transfer kinetics. The Tafel slopes at the lower overpotential region were 47, 60, and 59 mV dec−1 (Fig. 5). The Tafel slope of 47 mV dec−1 for CDCoNi-1 in the present case suggested a Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [62]. The mechanism involved surface-hydrogen bonding during the reaction. The method of extrapolating the Tafel slope (Figs. S10 and B) provided the data regarding the exchange current (ECD, J
0 ). The higher ECD indicates higher intrinsic catalytic activity [63]. Pt/C (0.017 mAcm−2) showed the highest ECD value followed by CDCoNi-1 (4 × 10−3 mA cm−2), 2 (3.1 × 10−3 mA cm−2), 3 (2.5 × 10−3 mA cm−2), and CDs (1.5 × 10−3 mA cm−2). CDCoNi-1 with the highest ECD value showed faster electrochemical charge transfer through the catalyst-solution interface. Table 1 lists the catalytic behavior of the as-prepared catalysts.

To gain further knowledge about the catalytic activity of the CDCoNi-n nanocomposites towards the mechanism of the HER, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies have been performed within the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. 6 A presents the Nyquist plot for CDCoNi-n and other catalysts. The equivalent electrical circuit (EEC) was designed from the Nyquist plot. The feasibility of the redox reaction at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces can be obtained from the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) that is proportional to the diameter of the arc [14]. A smaller diameter indicates a smaller charge transfer resistance, resulting in faster charge transfer between the catalyst and solution [63]. Among the three composites, CDCoNi-1 showed lowest Rct value of 170 Ω. The corresponding Bode diagram (Fig. 6B), representing the modulus of impedance (Z) and angle phase (ω) as a function of log of frequency (f), supports the Nyquist plot. The introduction of CD particles to the CoNi composite improved their electrochemical activity. Since, the CDs were N-doped, the unshared electron pair on the N-sites embedded in the carbon matrix would improve the active sites of the composite and their activities. In addition, the mono-metallic catalysts could not show significant activity. On the other hand, the metal content in the composite would influence their activity. The catalytic property was improved as the Co content was increased. The improved behavior can also be accounted for by measuring the electroactive surface area (ECSA). The ECAS (A) was estimated using Randles–Sevcik equation for the reversible process using the potassium ferrocyanide/potassium ferricyanide redox couple [64,65]. The slope of the anodic peak current (I
pa ) vs. the square root of the scan rate (ν
1/2 ) gave a value of A.

Cyclic voltammetry at different scan rates (10, 20, 40, 70, and 100 mV s−1) was performed with the CDCoNi-n composites in a 1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.05 M KCl solution. The D values for K3[Fe(CN)6] = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 [57] and n = 1 revealed estimated values of A for CDCoNi-1, 2, and 3 were 0.065, 0.060, 0.054 cm2, respectively. The results indicated the high catalytic activity of CDCoNi-1. Moreover, the intrinsic per-site activity in terms of the specific surface area (SSA) of a catalyst is an important measure for comparing the catalyst efficiency. To gain further insight regarding the catalyst performance towards the HER, the SSA of CDCoNi-n was measured using the following equation:
(7)

Where, J is the current density (−23.1, −8.3 and −9.9 mA cm−2 for CDCoNi-1, 2 and 3, respectively) at −0.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl); m is the mass loading (0.1 mg cm−2) of the catalyst, and S
BET is the BET surface area (46.909, 48.071, and 37.304 m2 g−1 for CDCoNi-1, 2 and 3, respectively). The magnitudes of the SSA were 0.49, 0.17 and 0.26 for CDCoNi-1, 2 and 3, respectively. This again indicated the higher activity of CDCoNi-1 towards the HER.

The application of an electrocatalyst is also dependent on its stability. The stability of the as-prepared electrocatalysts was investigated using the chronoamperometric (CA) method at a constant potential of −0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 10 h (Fig. 7 ). CDCoNi-1 showed ∼95% retention of the current. The SEM image of CDCoNi-1 (Fig. 7) showed that the composite maintained almost similar morphology even after 10 h of electrocatalytic reaction. CDCoNi-2 and 3 also showed almost unchanged morphology after 10 h of electrocatalytic reaction (Fig. S11). The XRD pattern shows a slight shift in the peak positions, but the (100), (101), (102) and (110) planes remain unaltered (Fig. S12). The XPS analysis showed that the Ni2p and Co2p peaks remained unaltered. Further deconvolution of Ni2p peaks indicated distinct presence of +2 and + 3 oxidation states with slight shift in 2p(+3) peaks. However, deconvolution of Co2p peak showed that the +3 and + 2 states in Co2p3/2 could not be clearly distinguished (Fig. S13). The spectroscopic measurements indicated structural and phase stability of the as-synthesized composites [66]. The synergistic interaction within the layered double hydroxide protected the morphology of the bimetallic double hydroxide [67]. Again, the CDs interacted with metals through the surface functionalities to provide structural stability.

A successful HER electrocatalyst should be performed efficiently in both acidic and alkaline media. To examine the activity of CDCoNi-n composites, LSV measurements were performed in different buffer media at pH = 4, 7 and 10. CDCoNi-1 exhibited similar behavior over the pH range (Fig. 8 ). The overpotential values at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, in pH = 4, 7, and 10 were −237, −240, and −240 mV, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively.. When converted with respect to RHE, the overpotential values become −28, −31, and −31 mV, respectively. However, CDCoNi-2 and 3 showed higher activity at lower pH than at higher pH. The Tafel slopes were calculated to be 49, 45, and 42 mV dec−1 at lower overpotential region. These values are revealed the similar activity of CDCoNi-1 over the pH range. Therefore, CDCoNi-1 could be used over a wide range of pH. The activity was examined further using 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (Fig. S14). The overpotential for CDCoNi-1 was −340 mV, vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−131 mV with respect to RHE), whereas that for CDCoNi-3 was −328 mV vs. Ag/AgCl electrode (−119 mV with respect to RHE). The Tafel slopes for CDCoNi-n, n = 1, 2, and 3 were 176, 163 and 155 mV dec−1, respectively. The results indicated that CDCoNi-3 had better activity than CDCoNi-1. The composite with a higher Co content worked better in the H2SO4 electrolyte, whereas composite with a higher Ni content showed better activity in the KOH electrolyte. From the above experimental data, it has been observed that CDCoNi-1 showed comparable activity in both acidic and alkaline medium. Co and Ni both being multi-electron system can act as efficient electrocatalyst depending on the morphology and functionality. Ni could provide low overpotential and Co could provide high hydrogen adsorption during the electrochemical process [68]. However, it was observed the at proper concentration ratio of the metals the optimum activity of the overall catalysts could be achieved. Previously, Baranton et al. [24] showed the catalytic activity of the Ni x Co 10-x catalysts increased with increased Co content. They correlated the activity with the stability of the catalysts. They compared the stability of Ni x Co 10-x catalysts in terms of dissolution of Co in solution. It was observed that Ni x Co 10-x catalysts with x ≤ 5 Ni provided stability by suppressing the Co dissolution rate in medium. The difference in catalytic activity in the different pH buffer solution indicated that Co dissolution was hindered in the presence of a lower Ni content. The modification of electronic environment around Co center improved the intrinsic catalytic property of Co-based electrocatalysts [69]. Digraskar et al. [70] reported that for the HER by a Ni based composite, the HER activity was weakened with increasing Ni loading. Furthermore, they found better activity of the catalyst in H2SO4 compared to KOH. Thus, the higher catalytic activity of the CDCoNi-1 with higher Co content and higher ECSA could be explained.
When compared with previous carbon dot-associated metallic composites, CDCoNi-1 showed comparable results towards the HER (Table 2 ).

The choice of catalyst for electrochemical HER is very important to achieve successful reaction. The catalyst should be stable, low-cost, still effective. Hence, search for better catalyst is still going on. In this regard, our work would provide more insight for designing efficient catalysts. In this work, CD templated metallic double hydroxide nanocomposites have been designed. Such nanocomposites for HER in acid electrolyte have not been reported so far to the best of our knowledge. The synergistic interaction within CDs and the metal counterpart was utilized for improved catalytic activity. CDs provided more active sites towards the metal hydroxide composite surface. Fig. 5 indicates that individual activity of CD or mono-metallic or bi-metallic catalysts were not significant compared to the CD-bi-metallic composite. The as-synthesized composites show significant stability with respect to morphology and activity even after 10 h of reaction. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of CDCoNi-1, over a wide pH range (1–12) for hydrogen evolution reaction, promises its successful application in energy field. Henceforth, this work would provide a significant contribution towards HER related experiments.

A series of CD-supported cobalt nickel hydroxide nanocomposites were synthesized in aqueous media using the low temperature hydrothermal treatment. The composite with a lower Ni content exhibited better stability and activity towards the HER. The presence of CDs increased the activity by providing a higher surface interaction and electronic environment. CDCoNi-1 with a higher Co content showed better activity towards the HER at a buffer solution over a wide range of pH. The over potential was -234 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. The high catalytic property was attributed to the interconnected structure of the nanocomposite. This work paves the way to design a zero dimensional carbon-supported metallic nanocatalyst at low cost for the HER and energy storage.

Jayasmita Jana: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Jin Suk Chung: Validation, Project administration. Seung Hyun Hur: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
